items.title: Man rescued alive from the rubble eight days after the twin earthquakes in Venezuela | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
<article>In Venezuela, rescuers have pulled a survivor from a collapsed building eight days after twin earthquakes struck the north of the country. The 43-year-old security guard had been trapped under rubble in the basement of a shopping center since the 24th of June. Rescuers worked for over 100 hours to free him, braving torrential rain and aftershocks. They kept him alive with food and water while excavating the concrete structure that collapsed around him. He was saved because his cabin held ground and created an air pocket. The back-to-back earthquakes destroyed tens of thousands of buildings and killed more than 2,200 people.

0:00 Search and rescue teams are still hoping to find more survivors under the rubble in Venezuela
0:44 Loyce Pace, Regional Director for the Americas at the International Federation of Red Cross and Red Crescent Societies

#dwcurrentaffairs #venezuela #rescue

items.title: Ukraine wants to export weapons - while still fighting Russia | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
<article>Ukraine has spent more than four years asking allies for weapons. Now Kyiv says it is ready to sell some of its own ‚ÄĒ drones, counter-drone systems and battlefield tech developed under fire. But after another heavy strike on Kyiv, the question is stark: can Ukraine export arms while still fighting for survival?

0:00 Can Ukraine start exporting weapons in the middle of the Russian onslaught?
1:14 DW Ukraine correspondent Nick Connolly in Kyiv
3:21 Nico Lange, Senior Fellow, Munich Security Conference, Founder and Director IRIS Institute for Risk Analysis and International Security

#dwcurrentaffairs #ukraine #russia

items.title: What does China's "Ethnic Unity Law" mean for its ethnic minorities and for Taiwan? | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
<article>China began implementing its new, so-called "Ethnic Unity Law" on July 1. Western governments have raised concerns over how Beijing could use the law to forcibly assimilate ethnic minorities and to prosecute people overseas. The Taiwanese government has warned its citizens that they could be targeted.

items.title: Is Russia opening a new front? Ukraine warns of northern offensive | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
<article>Is Russia preparing to launch a new offensive in northern Ukraine?
Ukraine's commander-in-chief, Oleksandr Syrskyi, says Kyiv is preparing for the possibility of a Russian attack from the Bryansk region. But he argues the objective would not be another assault on Kyiv. Instead, Russia could try to seize territory in northern Ukraine and force Kyiv to divert troops away from the fiercest fighting in the east.

At the same time, Syrskyi says Belarus is unlikely to allow its territory to be used as a launchpad for another invasion, despite repeated warnings from President Volodymyr Zelenskyy in recent weeks.

In this interview, we speak with Marina Miron, military analyst at King's College London, about:

Is a new Russian offensive really likely?
Why would Moscow open another front now?
Is this about capturing territory‚ÄĒor stretching Ukraine's defences?
How significant is Belarus' role today?
What does this tell us about Russia's broader military strategy?


#Ukraine #Russia #Ukrainewar #Bryansk #dwcurrentaffairs


For more news go to: http://www.dw.com/en/

items.title: Couple in Indonesia caned for kissing on TikTok livestream | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
<article>Aceh allows up to 100 lashes for morality offenses including adultery, gambling and gay sex. The region was granted permission to enforce religious law in the 2000s in a deal to end a separatist war. The local Sharia police chief Muhammad Rizal said it was the first time the province punished people for violating Sharia law on social media. 

#dwnews #caning #Indonesia

items.title: Merz unveils sweeping reforms to boost German economy | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
<article>Germany's coalition government has announced a wide-ranging package of pension, tax and labor reforms aimed at boosting economic growth and strengthening competitiveness. Chancellor Friedrich Merz said the measures would create jobs, reduce bureaucracy and preserve key social welfare protections. The 34-point plan includes income tax relief for low- and middle-income households as well as a gradual increase in the retirement age. The government hopes to pass the core reforms through parliament by the end of the year.

0:00 Berlin hails breakthrough on pension, tax reforms
0:17 What are the goals of the reform?
1:05 Germany‚Äôs reform package explained
2:33 DW Political Correspondent Simon Young
2:46 One of the most unpopular governments ever has to come up with something big
3:45 Do the measures address high bureaucracy and sluggish investment?
4:50 How fragile is the coalition‚Äôs unity behind the reform package?
5:54 There are hard pills to swallow for both parties

items.title: Russia launches massive overnight strike on Kyiv | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
<article>Kyiv came under one of the war‚Äôs longest aerial assaults, with Russian missiles and drones pounding the Ukrainian capital for 11 hours. At least 18 people were killed and dozens injured as residents took shelter and damage was reported across the city. Russia says the strike was retaliation for attacks on its infrastructure.

0:00 Russia launches missile, drone attack on Kyiv
0:26 Destruction in Kyiv after last night‚Äôs attacks
1:48 Ukraine‚Äôs own attacks have hit Russia‚Äôs energy infrastructure
2:17 DW correspondent Nick Connolly 
2:35 Relatively few Russian missiles were intercepted, there is a feeling of vulnerability
4:45 With Patriot stocks dwindling, Ukraine attacks Russia‚Äôs ballistic missile production facilities
6:10 Reactions from Ukraine's top politicians to Germany‚Äôs Nord Stream charges against a Ukrainian national
7:15 Kyiv waits for the current attention on Nord Stream to wane

items.title: 11-year-old crashes into group of Thai monks | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
<article>Driving his parents' truck, an 11-year-old boy crashed into a procession of Buddhist monks in northeastern Thailand, killing at least nine. They were part of a group of over 30 monks and five lay followers who were on a pilgrimage from one temple to another. #dwnews

items.title: Google loses fight against massive anti-trust fine | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
<article>Google has lost its battle against the biggest anti-trust penalty in European history. The European Court of Justice has upheld a record fine of more than 4.1 billion euros, rejecting the tech giant's appeal over what regulators say were anti-competitive business practices.

0:00 Smartphone manufacturers were required to pre-install Google Chrome and Google Search
1:03 Google was fined before by the EU
1:21 Trump accuses EU of targeting US tech firms
1:36 Matthias Kettemann, University of Innsbruck
1:47 How significant is this ruling?
2:15 Anti-trust fines are a last resort
2:51 Browser, smartphone and search engine markets are not competitive
4:00 It has fallen upon the EU to become the world‚Äôs digital regulator
5:01 Penalties won‚Äôt change corporate behavior, but Europe sends a signal: ‚ÄúYou have to respect our laws‚ÄĚ

items.title: Russia's biggest ever attacks hit Kyiv, but is it Ukraine that has the advantage | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
<article>Russia launched a major overnight aerial attack on Kyiv, killing several people and injuring dozens more. Russia's intensified attacks come as Ukraine's own drone campaign against Russian military sites and energy facilities has caused fuel shortages and disrupted supply lines there. Ukraine says the goal is to take the war to Russia and to get Vladimir Putin to agree to end the conflict. So despite these latest explosions rocking the Ukrainain capital, has something shifted in this long grinding war? Does Ukraine have the momentum? And is Volodymyr Zelenskyy pushing Putin into a corner he'll struggle to get out of?

For more news go to: http://www.dw.com/en/

items.title: Calling in sick is getting harder in Germany | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
<article>Chancellor Merz has criticized Germany's sick-leave rate as too high in the past and announced measures to bring it down ‚ÄĒ sparking a heated debate in the country.

Today, the governing coalition presented them as part of a broader reform package: ending sick‚ÄĎleave requests made by phone and introducing the requirement to obtain a doctor‚Äôs note from the first day of absence.
